text: So, I think it's safe to say that
Starfleet Academy hasn't exactly been
the resounding success that Paramount
were hoping for.
What with it failing to chart on the
Neielson ratings at all, crashing out of
Paramount's own top 10 within a few
weeks of release, the abysmal audience
scores on every major review site, and
the first episode released free on
YouTube gaining less views than an
average video of mine. And when the
worst happens and everyone's taken a
massive steaming dump on the uh massive
steaming dump that you've produced,
there's only one sensible course of
action. Cope. And by golly, did the cope
come hard and fast with this one. No.
You see, this series is actually good if
you look at it from a certain point of
view. This show wasn't made for stodgy
old Trek fans like you. It was written
for the next generation. People are just
reviewbombing this show because it's
diverse and progressive, not because
it's bad. Star Trek has always been
diverse and progressive. So, shut up and
don't question how this contradicts the
previous point. Just like what we tell
you to like and be happy, godamn it.
I've seen it all, man. Or at least I
thought I had. But this latest defense
has got to be the saddest and most
desperate move even I could have
imagined. Coming courtesy of Subspace
Chatter on Substack. quote, "It seems
people, let's call them the usual
suspects, began reviewbombing Star Trek
Starfleet Academy almost as soon as it
premiered a few weeks ago. These weren't
thoughtful critiques. These were folks
just reflectively and maliciously
slapping the new series with a minimum
one-star rating on review aggregator
sites like IMDb and Rotten Tomatoes.
Are you serious?" I mean, honestly, I
think giving it one star is being
generous because that implies the
presence of something worthwhile in the
show. no matter how small or deeply
buried. And well, that would be a lie.
Is Starfleet Academy perfect? Nope. Is
it too cheesy at times? Absolutely. But
if we keep complaining and tank its
rankings just because it's not how we
think Star Trek should be, the only
outcome is that it gets cancelled and we
get nothing else.
Whoa, whoa, whoa. Wait just a second
there, Missy. You're suggesting that
people should give this show a free pass
because if they're too mean to it, it
might kill off Star Trek altogether.
What you just said is one of the most
insanely idiotic things I have ever
heard.
>> I mean, you understand how insane that
is, right? Just accept the absolute dog
[ __ ] you've been served up on the slim
chance that you might be given something
slightly less [ __ ] as some indeterminate
point in the future.
>> How about new?
>> Call me crazy, but I tend to take the
opposite view on this kind of thing. If
a company produces a product that
basically everyone hates, it's important
to let them know just how badly they've
[ __ ] up. Otherwise, they're going to
keep doing it because you're giving them
no incentive to change. Starfleet
Academy, just like Section 31 and
Discovery before it, is exactly the kind
of show that Alex Curtsman and his gang
of hapless retards at Secret Hideout
want to make. This is their vision of
what Star Trek should be. And if you let
them get away with it through some kind
of benign indifference in the hopes
they'll get better by themselves, then
of course they'll keep making more of it
because it's exactly what they want to
do. They have to be corrected, sir. Fans
would be better served finding things in
the shows they do like and providing
constructive feedback on what they'd
like to see more of. Well, there in lies
your problem because I'll be honest
here. I can't find anything in this show
that I like. The characters, the
writing, the set design, the
performances, the emotional tone, the
themes and ideas, they all have about as
much in common with actual Star Trek as
I do with Rachel Zgler.
>> It's Hollywood, baby.
>> There are literally no redeeming
qualities about this show. Every episode
is nothing but 60 minutes of soul
sucking, brain destroying, morale
sapping misery. It's an absolute insult
to the Star Trek legacy, an insult to
the craft of film making and an insult
to basic human intelligence. And to
pretend otherwise is just flatout lying
to yourself. And well, why the [ __ ]
should you? The constant drum beat of it
all sucks and everyone needs to be fired
isn't going to result in anything
constructive. Instead, it's likely to
result in Paramount Shelvin Trek for
another 15 years.
Oh no. Oh dear. You're threatening me
with a good time now, aren't you? But
joking aside here, I think we've come
down to it at last. The crux of the
matter, the inescapable truth around
which this entire show folds. Like an
origami chicken.
>> [ __ ] ME, I can't believe a functional
human being wrote that line. Either way
though, the result is the same. It's
time to let Star Trek die. And believe
me, I take no pleasure in saying that,
but there it is. This franchise is
[ __ ] cooked, man. It's like a blind
old incontinent dog that can't walk
anymore. As much as you loved it and
treasure the good times you had
together, keeping it alive in this
condition isn't helping anyone. It's
just prolonging the inevitable. The
truth is that for all intents and
purposes, Star Trek died 20 years ago
with the cancellation of Enterprise. And
[ __ ] me, as stale and mediocre as that
show was at the time, it's a goddamn
masterpiece compared to anything we've
had since. Yeah, we've been treated to
some big flashy space action movies with
the Star Trek name plastered over them.
And we got a little nostalgic glimmer of
past glory with that final season of
Peicard. But for all intents and
purposes, the spirit of Star Trek died a
long time ago. All that's left now is a
shambling zombie franchise kept going
with increasingly desperate TV shows
that nobody asked for and even fewer
people enjoy. The idea of shelving this
franchise and just letting it rest for
10 or 15 years sounds like exactly what
it needs. It needs a [ __ ] break. It
needs time for the wounds of Curtsman
Trek to heal. It needs time for the
fandom to feel nostalgia for what once
was an anticipation for something new.
And more importantly, it needs time for
a new generation of writers and
creatives to take the helm. Because
nothing in Star Trek is going to change
unless you do something about the people
making it. No matter how you try to spin
it, the fact is that nothing good is
being accomplished by Curtsman and his
team anymore. After almost a decade in
control of this franchise, they haven't
grown the fandom. They haven't elevated
the Star Trek brand to greater cultural
awareness. And from a purely business
point of view, they haven't made a
single [ __ ] penny for Paramount.
Nobody's buying Star Trek merch or box
sets or taking out Paramount Plus
subscriptions to watch Star Trek
Discovery. It would be quicker to dump a
hund00 million dollars into a pile and
just light the [ __ ] thing on fire. At
least that would look cool. Normally, I
take no pleasure in the idea of people
losing their jobs, but in this case, I
don't see any other solution here. The
Secret Hideout guys have 100% brought
this on themselves. Nobody forced them
to make shitty Star Trek shows filled
with lorebreaking characters and
storylines. Nobody forced them to
prioritize the message over good
storytelling. And nobody forced them to
lash out and criticize the very fans
they needed to win over. They are
entering into the finding out stage of
the fuckaround process. And they've got
no one to blame but themselves. Star
Trek in its current form needs to die
because it's doing nothing except
tarnishing its own legacy by continuing
to exist. Maybe someday it can come back
stronger and better than before. But
let's be honest, that time is a long way
off. For now, I'd settle for it being
left to rest in peace.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today.
Go away now.
